speaker: interviewer
text: Well there's no reason to shut it off. You can say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I wanted to say, which I didn't want to put on it is that I am pretty strong, and I will finish it today. But I wonder whether in general you [INAUDIBLE] better to do it in two times. But you have you have your reason why not. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can help you in one area.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to say something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Am I ready? There is not much more to this romantic story. We made a date. I said to her I will be here tomorrow at this and this hour. Try to be here and, I could get you somewhere to whatever I can scratch together. And I came the next morning, as I said. But she wasn't there. And I came and asked all the women and said, I know you wait for whatever her name was-- Erica, Monica, something like this. She is just in a group of women going out of the camp. And she went somewhere for work. And of course I have no way of-- the end of the story. Because that was the end of the story, limited as it was. I have better stories if I only had better time.

words.text: if I only had better time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, please, please tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and sadder stories. But [CLEARING THROAT] there was a little-- first of all, I forgot to tell you that before the six months were over, the six months which my whole transport, which many of my best friends, including my secretary, all the other doctors, which were distributed to different barracks and didn't work at-- in the hospital, all went into the gas chamber. But before this happened-- it doesn't make sense, but nothing really makes sense. It is what happens. And if you happen to be lucky, and I am always lucky. I was already lucky when I stayed alive when I was born. But I am not bragging with it because there is some heaviness in it. --came another transport in our camp. And my parents were in this transport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a way, when they were-- when they heard that they go to this new camp where her son already is for half a year, they were happy. So finally we will get together. Of course, when they came to Birkenau, they were less happy. So what I did immediately, I tried to help them by giving them some clothes that I could organize. It's a word that was used there, and you either steal it, or get it in exchange with a favor to somebody else. And a doctor is always willing to give some favors maybe in the future. So if I come and say, do you have an old rag, or sweaters or shoes, they will give it to me. That was pretty hard. I mean, my father was then in his 50s, or maybe closer to 60. My mother was seven or six years  younger. But you know, for Birkenau that is already beyond the tolerable stage. My mother got immediately this terrible diarrhea, where the people died from it. But I could arrange her to be taken to the hospital, so she could last a little longer. My father, who is-- a creative-- was a very creative person, found immediately some activity for himself. People had lost their glasses. They were half blind. Through the [INAUDIBLE]. He took them in and said, we have some glasses. He found some glasses. He got glasses. And then, of course, there were ten thousands of glasses of people who were executed. You see, before they get into the gas chamber, not only the gold from the teeth, but everything else was taken. So he found a way to get some glasses, and-- and became active. And doing something was, from the psychological point of view, the most important thing to do. And not just to sit there and wait until they get you. And the 6th of March, when I was with my transport waiting to be executed, or transported to another camp-- either one, what you believe-- he was, as they were, of course very upset, my parents, to lose me. See, I mean I was a lifeline. But also their most grave concern-- what really would happen is the people who were transported away. When I came back the next day, of course they were extremely happy. It was not one day. It was two days and one night. And then the next night, in the middle of the next night, I was called back with Otto Heller, and Hans Hellman, and a few doctors and nurses that worked in the hospital. The patients were still in the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a second SS doctor. His name-- first name I forgot. His name was Klein. He spoke with a very Hungarian accent. He was a high SS [INAUDIBLE] major SS doctor. There is an enclave in Hungary-- I don't know any more whether it exists still-- where Germans lived. It was called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. Seven Citizens. I think seven citizens started it. And then it became a German colony in the middle of Hungary. But they grew up with two languages, Hungarian and German. And when the Nazis took over they threw away their Hungarian language-- not-- not the accent-- and spoke German, but ridiculously.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was more frequent than Mengele talking with us, with the patients. And he saw my father beaming, because I just had returned. And he said, what makes you so happy? And he said, Mein [? Herr, ?] Captain-- whatever he was-- Major Klein. And he said my son just came back from the transport and is again with us. When he says, oh it's always nice when family can stay together. Even though he would have it much better at the other-- would have had-- had it much better in the other camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a-- such a slimy, cynical creature. They caught him and hung him somewhere the day of liberation. But this-- it doesn't make a difference. They were all the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no Germans in your hospital, were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As patients?

speaker: interviewer
text: As patients.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They were only from our Jewish transport as it came from [NON-ENGLISH]. No, there were not. Not in this. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now the year '44, the year prior to the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Well, do you want to talk a little bit about the gypsy camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. It comes a little later.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '44, people were selected for work transport. Now we thought this was the same swindle again. They say work transport, and send them in the gas chamber. But this time it really were only the younger ones. They didn't even look at people over 50 --men as well as women. And the rumor was-- and it was true, which we [INAUDIBLE] later found out-- that they would go west into West Germany, where there was a project to build an enormous cave in the forest of Thuringian-- Thuringian-- I don't know what the English name is-- to create the atom bomb, which they nearly had already invented. And then the whole war will change. That was what every German there believed, and what was distributed as rumor. They need thousands and thousands of laborer to make that big hole in the mountain-- in the mountains of these forests. And therefore they sent in whoever was able still for hard work and little food. But then there were many who were too old. And they stayed back in our camp. I don't know, 6,000, maybe 5,000, 7,000, including my parents, including Willie Shoenfeld, the man with whom I had-- well, he wasn't old, but he was ill. He had had tuberculosis before, and it broke out again. And he big swollen glands. He was obviously a sick man. These old people were saying, I wonder what would happen, they said. But then there was also the hospital still, with the patients and a couple of, four doctors. Me one of [? seven. ?] In July, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] June, July '44, the big question was what will happen with the mothers with little children, which were in one barrack. And again, there came the orders the next morning-- we will transport some buses with little children to a special camp where they will be well taken care of. We didn't believe a word, but that was the official announcement. Next morning these 200, 300, [INAUDIBLE] more than 50 women with sometimes one child, sometimes two children on their hands were standing on the road waiting for what happens. We were clear what will happen. But of course, nobody gives up the total hope totally that something will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then something happens. Very little, but it happened. Suddenly one of the German guards-- many all around, so that nobody could escape-- had a-- how do you call it? Megaphone.

speaker: interviewee
text: --megaphone in his hand. And he announced, be clever woman. Be clever. Give your children to your neighbor and come out. And you can get in-- into last work transport, which will save your life, suddenly save your life. So everybody knew what will happen with the others. Two women did it. They gave the children to the neighbor. One of the women I knew and was not too surprised about, and one other one. All the other women went with their children in a slow [TAPPING RHYTHM] march to the gas chambers, and were never seen again. Then we knew what will happen with our camp. I-- again, I expected that this will be my end too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Unsure. Maybe it was the 12th of July. I don't know exactly-- 11th, 12th of July. One SS guard with a bicycle came to the hospital and called a few names-- Otto Heller. Hans Hellman was not there anymore. He already went with work, sneaked into one of the work transports. He was-- [COUGHING] Excuse me. And he was gone. Otto Heller, his wife who was a nurse, I, maybe one other doctor-- what's his name? Bernie-- Bernie-- Bernie Pollack. And he said, come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went up on his bicycle, and we walked. He went rather slow. Gave us some kind of piece of probably what will happen to us. We know too much. We had seen too much. That might be our end. He went with us out of our sector of the camp and in direction to the end of the camp where the crematorium and gas chambers were. That was pretty much-- he didn't talk to us. There was no way to ask him what was happening. You don't ask questions. You know, you'll get beaten or something. And then we passed a gate, which was a gate to the central hospital. There was a central hospital, not anymore belonging to any of these Jewish camps. And he made a left turn, opened the gate and said, go in. If it would have been further, we would have gone to the gas chambers. They wouldn't have opened the gas for four or five people. But [INAUDIBLE] it had single people there-- with-- put [? some rooms in ?] wall, [? and shoot us. ?] Again we were saved for the moment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The head of this camp was a Polish physician, a prisoner, but a privileged man-- a Polish military physician-- a great anti-Semite. There were [? really no ?] Jews there in this camp. And he said we have order to occupy you in our medical work here. And so we were alive. In this night, the whole Czech camp was [NON-ENGLISH] emptied out on trucks. And in the middle of the night we heard the trucks going to the gas chambers. Next morning the camp was empty. Smoke was all that remained. There were my parents, Willie Shoenfeld, all our patients-- this time seven. I mean we had fought to keep some people alive. And that was the end --and many others. I mean, there is no qualification who should or should not, or what it means. To me it meant, of course, tragedy. Because there I had lost my parents. I stayed in this camp for a few more months [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I heard that a transport from Holland [INAUDIBLE] arrived. And my fear was, of course, that my sister could be on it. Now I had met a Slovakian doctor in this central camp whose wife had a kind of position in the female camp, in the woman's camp. And so he had some contact with his wife. And she found out that my sister was at the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband apparently went directly into the gas chamber. We never knew why. He was a young man, healthy man, in his 30s. But maybe they asked people, are you sick? Are you healthy? And they may say sick, you know, thought to have an easier life in the camp. And they go off to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she went into the women's camp. She was, maybe 30, around. I could figure out. But a young woman. It was on October the 15th, which my birthday is. And she wrote me on a piece of paper that somebody gave her-- a little piece of newspaper-- newspaper-- happy birthday. Help me. I  am pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm pregnant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my sister. So through  the underground connection, I got this little message. And my friend who had his wife in the camp arranged with the doctor of the camp, said that she could have an abortion. Pregnancy was immediately death. First of all, it was forbidden to be pregnant. It was forbidden to give birth. But also these were women prepared for work situations. They didn't need pregnant women. She had a very bad abortion, with infection. And later on, in America, had many spontaneous abortions until she finally could hold one child. And this is [? how I-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was October the 15th, '44. A month later there came another order. A number of doctors was on a list. And we would be sent to the west to help in this big work, the tunnel in Thuringian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that was already end of October [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of '44, the last winter of the-- beginning of the last winter of the war. The transportation was a little simpler than what we had experienced before. There was one cattle car with a kind of bench, and maybe 20 doctors. But we could even sit down. We didn't have any luggage. We had a piece of bread. We had again a bucket in the corner. But we could keep the doors-- you know, the-- the cattle door-- open so that we had some air. And this long trip through all Germany, from Poland through Germany to-- to Berlin. Next station was Berlin. There we came to Sachsenhausen or Oranienburg-- I think Sachsenhausen. Sachsenhausen was one of the oldest and biggest concentration camps in-- outside of Berlin, in the middle of Germany. And there we were for a month in quarantine. Now this time it was a real quarantine. They wanted to be sure when we get out, and to work, that we don't carry lice, or fleas, or illnesses, and can work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got the showers, and disinfections, and got shots, and all kinds of things, not nearly enough food, but enough so that we survived-- one piece of bread, one little bit of soap, and something like a coffee substitute in the morning. Again, one month sharp. And we were put clean and quarantined, and healthy into this time a real train, second class. First class-- [INAUDIBLE] not because we were so great. But that's all they had available at the time. Only that these upholsteries were full of lice and fleas. So when we finally came to the destination where we are supposed to be clean, not to spoil the new camp, well we were scratching and itching and infected. I mean, nothing worked anymore. From there we were sent to Ohrdruf. Ohrdruf was where little outside camps, one, two, and three, which were in the woods, close to the forest, where in the morning we were sent out digging what should have become the great tunnel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, freezing. It was winter by then. We had no good clothes, and minimal food, full of fleas. There was a general saying there that you can do it for a maximum-- a maximum of four weeks. Then you are finished. And we know this. They said. And then comes the next transport for the next four weeks. People [INAUDIBLE]. I mean this we understood from all the little information. You know there are always some older persons who have some have some position, and know what the newcomers doesn't know. And so-- so we know, if we are lucky, that maybe the war ends. That could have easily happened, but it didn't. And really after four weeks, I had the famous kind of infection in my legs, which usually that get [INAUDIBLE] to general infections. And I knew that was it-- much longer I can not do this. I had all kinds of ideas what to do. But nothing was practical in there. So one morning I didn't go to work. I said, we'll see what happens. I can't anymore. [INAUDIBLE] is extremely strenuous and cold and hard. And in comes one of the doctors, Dr. Goldstein, who was in very early personnel of Auschwitz. He was a doctor. And he had-- he was from Poland, had started in Paris, and was with these early transports of foreigners transported to Auschwitz from Paris, or wherever he was-- and somehow survived all the critical moments and worked as doctor in Auschwitz. And had met me there in the last station, the central hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what's the matter with you? So I said, [INAUDIBLE] pretty much that's the end of my strength. I give up. He says, oh come on. I'll get to with the hospital. We need some doctors. The condition is catastrophic. We opened new camp. Tomorrow I bring you to [INAUDIBLE] services [INAUDIBLE] SS doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was dirty. So I used the rest of the day to go in the restroom and scratch my dirt from the face. I found an old Gillette blade, razor blade. I scratched my beard, off so that I would look a little-- I tried to clean the rags I was in. And next morning I went to the little ambulances, all they had there. And he was there. And it-- it was dark. They had only one little bulb in one corner. You hardly could see. And there was a tall, slim, man in uniform, a high officer, the SS doctor. And he introduced me. He says, this is Dr. Bloch. Right now I am, he said, a very good doctor who worked with me in the central camp in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man talked to me. I couldn't see his face. And said, that is a little bit different from the Schmidt Clinic in Prague, isn't it? He was a doctor who worked at the same clinic with me in Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: That could have been good or bad. It happened to be good. And he says, I'm glad you will help us. And I was saved again. You know, one of these little cross points in my journey. Then I worked as doctor again, which first of all, of course, I got a shower. I had to burn all my clothes. They gave me some rags. Dr. [INAUDIBLE] was suddenly a respectable person again. And from there to the end of the war, I was really saved from any disasters-- until the front-- the American front, came closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were little episodes. What? How much time do we have? About 40 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 Minutes. Occasionally they would send us-- even though we worked as doctors-- out for work. One day I was sent out with a small group of colleagues and other prisoners in the camp, to a train station to unload food. I can't even tell you what it is-- what it was. It's the food that in peacetime they feed the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pigs with. But at that time, they made soup out of it. Well I don't really know  the name of it. Unloading--

speaker: interviewee
text: --turnip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Turnip. Thank you. So we worked, taking it out. We worked hard. But it was no big thing. It happened to be, even though it was winter, a sunny day. And the forest, it was like-- like being on vacation. It didn't matter too much. And suddenly there came alarms. The Americans came and bombarded the neighborhood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now in alarm, you have to, as a prisoner you can not get into the underground security places. You just stay in the place, and don't move.
